quiz.title: Macroeconomics (Quiz 9)
quiz.subtitle: Unit 5 Quiz 9: Fiscal Policy, Debt, Laffer Curve, and Automatic Stabilizers
quiz.subject: Economics
quiz.topic: Supply-Side Tax Cuts, National Debt, Real After-Tax Interest Rate, Laffer Curve, Cyclical vs Structural Deficit, Automatic Stabilizers

questions.question: An income tax cut that improves the incentive to work will have which of the following outcomes?
options: It will increase potential GDP and raise the after-tax real wage rate.
It will increase potential GDP, but not change the after-tax real wage rate.
It will not change potential GDP, but the real wage rate will increase.
It will increase employment, but not change the real wage rate.
It will not change potential GDP, but employment will increase.
questions.explanation: A supply-side tax cut that improves work incentives increases labor supply. This shifts the LRAS curve rightward, increasing potential GDP. Workers also keep more of each dollar earned, so the after-tax real wage rate rises.

questions.question:
At the end of 2020, the country of Arcania has a national debt of zero. Over the next four years, the government records the following annual budget results:

2021: A deficit of \$150 billion
2022: A deficit of \$90 billion
2023: A surplus of \$60 billion
2024: A deficit of \$30 billion

Assuming the government must pay interest on any outstanding debt held at the beginning of each year, what will the total national debt of Arcania be at the end of 2024?
options: \$210 billion
\$240 billion
\$330 billion
Greater than \$210 billion, depending on the interest rate
Greater than \$330 billion, depending on the interest rate
questions.explanation: The simple sum of deficits and surpluses yields \$210 billion (150 + 90 - 60 + 30 = 210). However, because Arcania carried debt from 2021 through 2024, interest would accrue on the balance each year. This interest adds to the total amount owed, meaning the final debt must be higher than the simple sum. The exact amount depends on the interest rate.

questions.question: Suppose the government sets the tax rate on capital income at 40%. The market currently has a real interest rate of 5% per year, and the inflation rate is 2% per year. What is the real after-tax interest rate in this economy?
questions.explanation: Nominal rate $i = 5\% + 2\% = 7\%$. After-tax nominal rate $= 7\% \times (1 - 0.40) = 4.2\%$. Real after-tax rate $= 4.2\% - 2\% = 2.2\%$.

questions.question: Suppose the tax rate on capital income is 30%. The economy is experiencing a real interest rate of 1% per year, but inflation is significantly higher at 6% per year. What is the real after-tax interest rate for an investor in this economy?
questions.explanation: Nominal rate $i = 1\% + 6\% = 7\%$. After-tax nominal rate $= 7\% \times (1 - 0.30) = 4.9\%$. Real after-tax rate $= 4.9\% - 6\% = -1.1\%$. The investor actually loses purchasing power despite earning interest.

questions.question: Raising the tax rate up along the Laffer curve creates which of the following outcomes?
options: Tax revenue always increases.
Tax revenue always decreases.
Tax revenue does not change.
Tax revenue increases to a peak and then decreases.
Tax rate has no effect on tax revenue.
questions.explanation: The Laffer curve illustrates that tax revenue increases as tax rates rise from 0%, reaches a maximum at some optimal rate, and then declines as rates approach 100% due to disincentives to work and invest.

questions.question: The nation of Southland is currently in a deep recession. The government reports a Total Budget Deficit of \$50 billion. Economists calculate that if the economy were at full employment, the government would actually have a budget surplus of \$10 billion. What is the cyclical deficit in Southland?
options: \$40 billion
\$60 billion
\$50 billion
\$10 billion
-\$60 billion
questions.explanation: Total Deficit = Structural Deficit + Cyclical Deficit. A surplus is a negative deficit. So: \$50 billion = (-\$10 billion) + Cyclical Deficit. Cyclical Deficit = \$50 + \$10 = \$60 billion.

questions.question: The nation of Valoria is experiencing a significant economic expansion. The government reports a Total Budget Surplus of \$20 billion. However, internal audits show that the government's Structural Deficit is \$5 billion. What is the cyclical component of Valoria's budget?
options: A cyclical deficit of \$15 billion
A cyclical surplus of \$15 billion
A cyclical surplus of \$25 billion
A cyclical deficit of \$25 billion
Zero
questions.explanation: Total Balance = Structural Balance + Cyclical Balance. +\$20 = -\$5 + Cyclical Balance. Cyclical Balance = \$20 + \$5 = \$25 billion. Since positive, it is a cyclical surplus—typical during expansions when tax revenues are high and welfare spending is low.

questions.question: As the economy enters a recession, which of the following events occur automatically?
options: Tax revenues rise and interest payments on the debt rise.
Tax revenues and transfer payments rise.
Government outlays rise and tax revenues fall.
Government outlays tend to fall and tax revenues fall.
Interest payments on the debt rise and tax revenues fall.
questions.explanation: Automatic stabilizers kick in during a recession: falling incomes reduce tax revenues, and rising unemployment increases government outlays on programs like unemployment benefits. This happens automatically without new legislation.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a supply-side fiscal policy?
options: An increase in government spending on unemployment benefits
A cut in the marginal tax rate on interest income
An increase in the overnight rate target by the central bank
A one-time stimulus check sent to all households
A reduction in the federal funds rate
questions.explanation: A cut in the marginal tax rate on interest income is a supply-side fiscal policy because it increases the after-tax return on saving. This encourages capital accumulation and investment, shifting the long-run aggregate supply curve rightward and increasing potential GDP. Increases in transfer payments and stimulus checks are demand-side fiscal policies that boost aggregate demand. Changes to policy interest rates are monetary policies, not fiscal policies.

questions.question: Which of the following actions is an example of expansionary fiscal policy?
options: An action that increases aggregate demand.
An action that decreases aggregate demand.
An action that increases short-run aggregate supply.
An action that increases long-run aggregate supply.
An action that increases unemployment level.
questions.explanation: Expansionary fiscal policy—such as tax cuts or increased government spending—aims to increase aggregate demand to close a recessionary gap and boost real GDP and employment.

questions.question: During a business-cycle expansion, how does the automatic stimulus influence the inflationary gap?
options: The inflationary gap worsens as budget revenues fall and outlays increase.
The inflationary gap shrinks as budget revenues rise and outlays decrease.
Budget revenues increase with no change in outlays, so the inflationary gap worsens.
The cyclical surplus created by the government budget decreases.
The structural surplus created by the government budget decreases.
questions.explanation: During an expansion, automatic stabilizers work in reverse: rising incomes boost tax revenues, and lower unemployment reduces transfer payments. This creates a contractionary fiscal drag that shrinks the inflationary gap.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You have mastered supply-side tax policy, debt dynamics, real after-tax returns, the Laffer curve, and automatic stabilizers.
scoreMessages.70: Good work! You understand the core fiscal policy concepts. Review the distinction between cyclical and structural deficits.
scoreMessages.50: A fair attempt. Focus on: 1) Real after-tax rate = (nominal × (1 - t)) - inflation. 2) Total deficit = Structural + Cyclical. 3) Automatic stabilizers dampen the business cycle.
scoreMessages.0: Keep practicing! Remember: Supply-side tax cuts shift LRAS right. Inflation erodes after-tax returns. The Laffer curve shows revenue peaks at an optimal rate, not 100%.
